desc:
A low mound covered with with short and springy grass rises above the
surrounding plains. The earth beneath the grassy cover is not as tightly packed
as in the flatter areas, suggesting that this mound may be of artificial origin.
Some distance to the north, the dark green line of the dense hedge alongside the
Old East Road can be discerned.
name: Grassy Mound

desc:
The ever-present gale deters anything tree-like from growing up here on this
high hill so the area is covered in spiny grasses and heather. On bright days,
the red of the flowers blooming among the furze bushes across the fields to the
north blaze against the darker green of their leaves.
name: On Top of a Hill

desc:
The fields you are standing in are in a pretty bad shape. All around you are
knee-high weeds, mostly red poppy. Nothing of worth seems to grow here. The soil
is free of stones, some of which probably have been used to build a citadel that
now lies in ruins east of here. Beyond this, you see wastelands as far as the
eye can see. South of here, a high hill strives towards the skies.
name: Mistreated Fields

desc:
As the hedge is planted in an east-westwards fashion here, the trail you are
upon takes you in the same directions. South of here lie old farmer fields, long
since forgotten. A few poppies of the type that grow in the fields glow red here
in the underbrush of the hedge, seeds blown here by southern winds.
name: Hedge Trail

desc:
To the south a largish mound, too regularly shaped to be of natural origin,
rises from a flat area among the downs. The grass here is soft and pleasant and
small yellow flowers are interspersed among the green. Some distance to the
north, the dark green line of the Great Hedge can be seen from the tops of most
of the low hills.
name: South Downs

desc:
What once must have been a mighty citadel lies in ruins here. Large stoneblocks
overgrown with moss and grass lie in a circle around a large hole. The hole is
well ten yards deep and thirty yards wide. It must once have been the cellar or
prison of the citadel, as the walls and floor are covered with stone. A path
leads north from here towards the hedge, and it looks as if it has been traveled
quite frequently lately.
name: Ruins of a Citadel

desc:
You are upon a battlefield of old, with rusted weapons and armours still lying
half-buried in the grass. Here and there bones stick out of the ground, as if
only a thin layer of soil was thrown upon the corpses in a vain attempt to
protect them from animals.
name: An Old Battlefield

desc:
These are desolate lands. The forbidding earth that covers these plains will not
allow more than low grass and writhing bushes to grow. You can spot some smoke
to the north, and the light of a campfire catches your eye.
name: The Wastelands

desc:
Hard, dark earth covers the ground around here, turning more and more into sand
further east. Grey grass grows in the cracks, as well as a few crooked bushes.
These dusky plains continue both west and east, while the lands to the north are
more travelled, and many paths seem to criss-cross over the terrain there.
name: The Wastelands

desc:
The ground here is fairly sandy, with more hard earth to the west. A few
hardened bushes grow in the vicinity, the last ones you can spot for a few miles
when looking eastwards. Far to the north the hedge runs all along the horizon.
name: The South Downs

desc:
Pale yellowish white sand covers these downs, with sparse tufts of grey grass.
Except for snakes and worms, little wildlife care to live in these surroundings.
The downs continue east and west, while the hedgetrail makes a turn north of
here, following the hedge closely.
name: The South Downs

desc:
Travelling among these sandy downs is fairly strenuous. The ground slips away
beneath the feet, leaving deep tracks. Both east and west these sandy hillplains
continue, while the green outlining of the hedge can be seen a few miles north.
name: The South Downs

desc:
Many confusing paths and trails run in different directions around here. Some
are manmade, other natural animal trails. It is hard to follow the hedgetrail at
times, as you mix it up with sidetracks disappearing into the low brush. The
hedge runs northwest though, and you can trust on the trail following it
closely.
name: Bend in the Hedge Trail

desc:
You have reached something of a crossing of paths here in the middle of the
plain, the widest leading through the grass northwards towards the hedgetrail.
Where the others lead is hard to tell, as they seem to go in circles or lead
straight out to empty plains.
name: The X-Crossing

desc:
The hedge avoids the sandy south downs by turning north and east here, and the
trail goes a few yards away from the thorny wall. A mile or two to the south and
west the hard dark earth around here turns into bright sand, where nothing seems
to grow.
name: Bend in the Hedge Trail

desc:
The trail is a few yards wide here, running upon hard dark brown earth. Looking
south you see brighter plains, covered mostly with sand. The hedge north of you
is high and thick, and you hear sounds of small animals from within.
name: Hedge Trail

desc:
These are the south downs, empty, sandy and devoid of most wildlife. Sandworms
and slithering snakes care to make a living here, feeding upon whatever small
animals stray into the area. These sandy downs continue southwards, while more
desolate land lies to the east and west, south of the hedge and the hedgetrail.
name: The South Downs

desc:
Small paths leave in almost all directions here, carved in the grassland. Should
you choose to follow one you would either end up in the sandy downs to the
south, on the hedge trail north of here, or in the plains that continue both
east and west.
name: The Scissors

desc:
Someone has built a shelter from old logs and grass here, providing some
protection from both sun and rain. The grass is fresh, and it is clear that it
is constantly maintained. There is a huge campfire in the middle of this dry
place. Remains of small animals lie in a heap behind the shelter, and a foul
stench emerges from there.
name: Old Camp

desc:
Old stone stairs lead down to this wrecked cellar, with pillars and partly razed
walls still standing. Everything down here is covered in stone, overgrown with
moss in places and with grass growing in cracks. An odd, cold feeling is over
this site.
name: Cellar of a Citadel

desc:
Dank and narrow crypts wind here below the citadel and down under the plains.
Graves full of bones lie in hollows at your sides, dug into the soft earth.
Being only about one yard wide at their most narrow passages, wielding a weapon
has its problems at times. Slowly winding eastwards, the tunnels get colder and
the air gets thicker.
name: Crypts of a Citadel

desc:
This is at the eastern end of the narrow tunnels dug beneath the citadel to
serve as a final restingplace for its inhabitants. A small chamber is here,
perhaps once serving as a room to mourn in and spend some time revering your
ancestors. The room is simply dug out of the earth, with no decorations except
for a few wooden benches along the soft earthen walls and a large stone slab
with many delicate inscriptions firmly set in the north wall. Filled with old
mourning and grief, the air here is thick and chilly.
name: Crypts of a Citadel

desc:
The deepest chamber of the crypts is large and paved with stone. Several
mansized stone chests are lined upon slabs on the floor, and the walls are all
covered with sealed graves as well. This must be the tomb where the commanders
and rulers of the citadel were buried. A faint green glow lights up the place,
but no such light source can be found.
name: Deep inside the Crypts

desc:
As a trustworthy companion, the trail follows the hedge closely in every small
twist and turn. Like an elder brother the hedge guides the trail west and east
towards bree and the lands beyond Weathertop. South of here lie bleak sandy
plains, the south downs.
name: Hedge Trail

desc:
Around here the downs are somewhat sandy and hilly, with bleached grass growing
in sparse tufts. Small traces on the ground gives evidence of snakes that
inhabit the area, dousing on the small flat rocks on hot sunny days.
name: The South Downs

desc:
The gentle hills of the downs to the south become a little steeper to the
southeast. To the north, the Great Hedge blocks the Old East Road from sight,
though it might be possible to glimpse it from the large hill to the southeast.
The trail continues north and east in the shadow of the hedge, following the
line layed out by the Old East Road.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
Gently rolling hills extend to the south all the way to the horizon. Some of the
hills are dotted by stands of small trees, stunted and twisted by the wind, but
by and large the thick green grass holds sway here.
name: South Downs

desc:
The landscape is very monotonous, as in every direction only rolling treeless
hills can be seen. The hills are green with thick grass -- in places cropped by
sheep -- and large clover patches. There is little shelter from the wind that
blows almost constantly, and this may account for the lack of trees.
name: South Downs

desc:
Throughout this area, the hillsides are formed of many flat and grassy terraces.
Each of the shelves lies approximately ten feet over the one below, and it is
unclear whether or not these steps are of natural origin. The ground is covered
with thick, spongy grass that resiliently springs back after being trodden upon,
along with patches of clover and dandelions.
name: Grassy Terrace

desc:
The ground is carpeted here by a tough grass that resiliently springs back
upright after each footstep. In some places, the grass has been closely cropped
-- probably by sheep -- while elsewhere it flourishes up to three feet in
height. To the south, the rolling hills become more pronounced, while to the
north the ground slopes gently downward until it is lost in the hazy distance.
name: South Downs

desc:
Winds whip across this hilly landscape, unobstructed by any obstacles. The low
and rounded hills are carpeted with thick grass, and the hissing of the wind
through the grass provides an almost constant background noise. From the tops of
the hills, the dark line of the Great Hedge can be spied to the north -- beyond
which lie the Old East Road and the Midgewater Marshes.
name: Pastoral Hillock

desc:
The thick grassy carpet covering the hills has been closely cropped by grazing
animals. Sometimes the tops of the hills have bare patches, and the chalky soil
is revealed. To the south the hills become rockier and crowd together, forcing
anyone travelling that direction to enter a narrow, winding canyon.
name: South Downs

desc:
This ridge is higher than most of the surrounding hills, and to the north it
terminates in a high rounded knob. The grass resembles that of a well-kept lawn
in a peaceful town, as it has been cropped closely by some type of grazing
animal -- probably sheep judging by the shortness of the remaining grass.
name: Grassy Ridge

desc:
To the west, the hills rise up and form a low ridge that runs north and south.
In every other direction, the green slopes rise and fall until lost from sight.
The grass is soft and spongy, and judging from the numerous spoor, it provides
an ideal haven for a number of small animals.
name: South Downs

desc:
Among these lonely and windswept hills, the only sounds, save for the constant
rustling of the plush grass, are the sporadic calls of birds and the distant
bleating of sheep. In every direction the hills continue, like waves on the
ocean.
name: South Downs

desc:
A deep and placid pond of clear water nestles here among the lush grassland. A
few small trees have taken root nearby, providing comfortable shade from the sun
during the day. In every direction, the rolling hills of the South Downs
stretch, becoming drier and more brown to the east.
name: Small Pond

desc:
The grass-covered hills of the South Downs rise on all sides in lonely
splendour. Patches of coloured wildflowers dot the green carpet of the lush
grass, giving the landscape an air of serenity. East and west, small ponds in
low places among the hills can be seen, providing fresh water for the thirsty
traveller.
name: South Downs

desc:
The hedge grows close to the side of a large hill to the south, and the trail
passes closely between the two. The hedge is not quite as dense here as at other
places, as the plants have not flourished on the rocky soil. It quickly recovers
its normal thickness both east and west though.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
The gentle hills of the South Downs roll away toward the southern horizon in a
panoramic vista. To the north and east, the Great Hedge and the trail that runs
on its southern flank wends its way between Bree and Weathertop. The grass is
very lush, and often is mingled with large patches of small yellow flowers or
clover.
name: South Downs

desc:
The hillsides here are terraced, with each of the grassy shelves sitting about
ten feet over the one below it. It is unclear whether this is a natural
phenomenum, or whether it is due to some artificial construction. The grass is
thick and spongy, seeming to promise bliss to those who will remove their shoes
and run carefree through it.
name: Grassy Terrace

desc:
This small mound is very symmetrical and crowned by a few stones that may have
been quarried and carried here. Whatever once stood here has long been claimed
by time and erosion. Directly to the east, a high hill at the north end of a
grassy ridge rises. It should be possible to see much of the land from that
vantage point.
name: Grassy Mound

desc:
The crown of this hill is several hundred feet higher than the surrounding
rolling grasslands. From this vantage point, the Old East Road can be clearly
espied as it passes some four or five miles to the north, beyond the dark green
line of the hedge. Farther north -- beyond the road -- a low white mist hangs
over the trackless Midgewater marshes.
name: High Hill

desc:
To the west, a high hill rises proudly over the others. It lies at the northern
edge of a ridge that runs to the south. Just to the north, the seldom-used trail
along the southern edge of the Great Hedge can be made out. In the grass, there
are numerous signs that rabbits, foxes, and other small animals have flourished
among the lonely hills.
name: South Downs

desc:
Here among the lonely hills of the South Downs, rabbits and many other small
animals have found an ideal home in the tall grass. In some places, the grass
looks as if it has been clipped, so evidently some grazing is done here --
probably by sheep occasional led here by their shepherd.
name: South Downs

desc:
The grassy slopes of the South Downs undulate gently toward the horizon. To the
south, there is a small depression filled with a pond. Northward, the dark green
line of the Great Hedge can be made out, with the Old East Road presumably on
the other side.
name: South Downs

desc:
The rich grass is criss-crossed by trails left by scampering animals. There are
signs that sheep graze here occasionally, as several of the hills have had their
grassy covering shorn close to the ground. To the north, the dark green line of
the Great Hedge can be made out, beyond which runs the Old East Road.
name: South Downs

desc:
The gentle hills wear a cover of thick grass interspersed with patches of
clover. Where the soil is exposed, it is a rich black loam with sporadic
outcroppings of chalky limestone. The wind rustling through the grass is the
only sound heard for hours at a time, along with the rare calls of lonely birds
and the bleating of distant sheep.
name: South Downs

desc:
This flat area is overgrown with a soft carpet of clover. The hills surround
this place like the edges of a bowl, proving shelter from the strong winds. A
small pond is nestled in one corner of the depression, and it is filled with
clear spring water.
name: Field of Clover

desc:
This trail runs parallel to the Old East Road, separated from it by the Great
Hedge. Eastward the land begins to climb gently upward, until finally the
Weather Hills are reached. South of here, the grassy slopes of the South Downs
lie, seemingly empty of all but grass.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
Running parallel to the Old East Road, but separated from it by the Great Hedge,
this trail skirts the northern end of the South Downs. The hills to the south
are carpeted with rich grass, and look to be an ideal environment for a great
many small animals.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
This trail runs along the southern side of the Great Hedge, mirroring the Old
East Road between Bree and Weathertop. To the south, the gentle hills of the
South Downs begin, stretching like a green sea to the southern horizon.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
Directly to the north, the twisted vegetation of the Great Hedge blocks access
to the Old East Road and to the Midgewater Marshes beyond. The trail here along
the southern edge of the hedge is in places completely overgrown, as most
travellers prefer to use the Old East Road. Southward, the grassy hills of the
South Downs begin, and they extend in that direction as far as the eye can see.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
This almost-overgrown trail runs east and west along the southern edge of the
Great Hedge. To the south, empty and grass-covered hills begin and continue
until lost from vision. Beyond the hedge lies the Old East Road, presumably, but
the vegetation is much too thick to see it.
name: Trail along the Hedge

desc:
Yard-high fields of grass seem to stretch in every direction without end. The
ground slopes slightly upward to the north, eventually falling off as an abrupt
cliff, hidden by the tall dense grass which perpetuates the illusion that the
field extends in that direction. The cliff forms one wall of a narrow valley
whose bed lies several dozen yards below.
name: Tall Grass before a Cliff

desc:
Steep slopes flank a narrow valley that descends from west to east. Swathes of
reddish brown trace clay and sandstone layers exposed by long ages of erosion.
The valley floor is covered in brambles and thistles growing on thin, ochre soil
that is mixed with stones and pebbles. A few scattered pine trees grow in this
infertile ground, their squat and spindly stature a testimony to their struggle.
name: Clay-lined Valley

desc:
Lush, green grass thrives in gently sloping fields which descend from west to
east. Numerous, brightly-hued flowers peek above the stalks of grass, eager to
peddle their nectarine wares to bees hovering over the meadow. Elder shrubs,
bearing brilliant white flowers, grow in dense clumps dotting the slopes.
name: Vibrant Meadow

desc:
The hillside here slopes gently downward from southwest to northeast. Thick,
dark-green grass covers most of the land, but is broken in many places by
exposed rocks of deep grey and pale brown. The rocks become less apparent toward
the bottom of the slope as the layer of soil thickens.
name: Sloping Grasslands

desc:
A low ridgeline runs to the northwest and southeast. This appears to be its
lowest point for miles around, as the ridge rises in both directions. The
western face of the ridge is very steep and bare of any vegetation, while the
eastern slope is much shallower and coated with thick grass. A flowery field can
be seen at the base of the ridge.
name: Low Ridge

desc:
This ridge in the downs gradually rises as it extends to the south. A steep,
nearly vertical scarp drops away to the west, revealing a green valley below but
offering no way to reach it. The eastern face also drops quickly, but levels out
to a more moderate slope after only a few hundred yards. The ridge is bare of
most growth, with only a few thin weeds sprouting from cracks in the rock.
name: Tall Ridge

desc:
This ridgeline is bare of all vegetation, revealing faded brown stone. It turns
gradually eastward as it progresses to the south. The western face of the ridge
is steep and fragmenting, allowing no safe passage in that direction. A layer of
soil has formed on the shallower eastern face, allowing short grass and tall
wildflowers of white and orange to take root.
name: Barren Ridge

desc:
This room is small and unadorned, but very tidy. A door in the west wall leads
outside, allowing the farmhands to come and go without disturbing the farmer and
his family. An open doorway least east into the main house, while a narrow
ladder extends through a hole in the ceiling. A small table and a couple of
chairs are almost the only furnishings.
name: Farmhands' Quarters

desc:
This farmhouse is much larger than most of its kind, and is divided into several
rooms. Open doorways lead west and east, while a door of pine that has been
stained a pale golden colour stands in the south wall. The floor is made from
heavy wooden boards, but much of it is covered in a thick woolen carpet. A
fireplace has been set at an angle in the southeast corner.
name: Large Farmhouse

desc:
This kitchen is a mess. A wide counter runs almost the full length of the east
wall. It is covered in half-prepared food and dirty pots and pans. Shelves and
cabinets line half of the south wall and much of the west. They bear a wide
variety of items that can be incorporated into meals, but there seems to be no
rhyme or reason to how things are arranged. Small windows in every wall but the
west allow in a good amount of light and breeze.
name: Messy Kitchen

desc:
This bedroom is much larger than those found in most farmhouses. A broad bed
skilfully crafted from dark wood sits against the east wall. A narrow table
stands opposite it, bearing a comb, razor, and a pair of brushes. A polished
sheet of metal is attached to the wall above it to serve as a mirror. An angled
wall of bricks fills the northeast corner, no doubt part of a fireplace and
providing some heat to this room on chilly winter nights. A polished wooden door
leads into the main room, while a narrow staircase leads upward.
name: Spacious Bedroom

desc:
This room is large, but its ceiling is low and sloped, coming to within two feet
of the floor at the east and west walls. Two large windows stand open in the
south wall, allowing in plenty of fresh air, as well as ample light during the
day. Two small but comfortable-looking beds are set up near the north wall, with
a small box of toys set between them.
name: Airy Loft

desc:
This tight room is squeezed between a plain wooden floor and the sloping sides
of the peaked roof overhead. A single window in the western wall stands open,
but stout shutters on either side stand ready to close off the room from the
weather. Two narrow cots fill most of the available area. A ladder leads down
through a hole in the floor to the room below.
name: Small Loft

desc:
This narrow valley bends in a gentle arc, growing slowly deeper as it extends
from west to south. The walls on either side are layered in bands of faded brown
and orange, highlighted by a few thin streaks of black. The floor of the valley
is mostly smooth, but care must be taken when moving to avoid fallen rocks that
litter the ground.
name: Curving Valley

desc:
To the north, this canyon bends gently and rises, but as it proceeds further to
the south, it twists and winds until making a sudden turn eastward. The walls of
the canyon are bare, exposing hundreds of layers of sediment compressed into
solid rock over the ages. A broken jumble of rocks lies as the bottom of a stone
shelf in the southern wall.
name: Winding Canyon

desc:
Vertical stone walls rise high on either side, allowing no sunlight to reach
this area. The ground is littered with stones of every shape and size, from tiny
pebbles to enormous boulders. A thin layer of sand has drifted between the
rocks, helping to smooth out the surface at least a little.
name: Deep, Shadowy Canyon

desc:
The walls of the canyon encroach from either side, leaving a path between them
as little as twenty feet across at times. The high, winding sides of the rift
leave this area is permanent shadow, and nothing grows here. The ground is
uneven, rising and falling seemingly at random and sloping a little bit towards
the west.
name: Narrow Canyon

desc:
This shelf of rock is situated about halfway up the canyon wall. Solid walls
surrounds it on three sides, while to the north a jumble of broken rocks leads
deeper into the canyon. A large pile of sticks and twigs has been woven into a
huge nest, and judging from the number of fresh bones lying scattered all over
the ground, it may still be occupied.
name: Rocky Ledge

desc:
The canyon makes a broad curve here, running roughly east and south. Tall walls
of solid rock rise on either side, exposing countless layers of colourful bands
lying atop one another. A trickle of water runs down the northwest wall, but it
provides just enough moisture for a long curtain of moss to grow the entire
height of the wall.
name: Bending Canyon

desc:
A thick layer of sand and dust coats the floor of the canyon, and it swirls and
twists into miniature dust-devils any time the wind courses past. High stone
walls rise toward the sky, blocking out all but a narrow strip overhead, and
keeping this area in heavy shadow even during the day. The main canyon runs to
the west and the south, but a jagged crack also offers a way to proceed north.
name: Dry Canyon

desc:
The canyon is very deep and narrow here, squeezed between solid stone walls. In
some places it is as little as a dozen feet across. The walls angle slightly
outward as they rise, but still allow only a narrow ribbon of sky to be seen,
and prevent any sunlight from reaching the canyon floor. Chips and shards of
fallen rock litter the ground, but few larger stones can be seen.
name: Narrow Canyon

desc:
A rocky ridge runs in from the west, where it has turned away from the main
north-south line of downlands to flank the northern slope of a valley. The
descent to valley is nearly vertical; the exposed rock face is smooth and
layered in white chalk near the top, with red and brown sandstone below. Wind
and rain have weathered the top of the ridge, rounding it out. The soil, though
thin and dry, is enough to support grass which blankets the ground.
name: Ridge above a Canyon

desc:
A gentle dip slope, stretching eastward from a high ridge to the west, sinks
here to form a shallow depression. Lush grass grows in the fertile soil that has
gathered here. Mounds of upturned soil betray numerous burrows that hide in this
hollow.
name: Dimple on a Slope

desc:
This barn is far larger than most, and provides more than ample room to house
the farm's animals as well as store hay, seed, and other provisions. Several
stalls line the northern wall, all showing signs of recent occupation. Mounds of
hay are stacked on the south half of the barn, with a fence running down the
centre to separate the hay from any animals that might escape their stalls.
name: Spacious Barn

desc:
The canyon winds and twists through a sinuous route, tending primarily north and
west from here. Tall walls rise on either side, the gap between them too deep
and narrow to allow any direct sunlight to enter this place. Pale lichen clings
to the southern wall, drinking in the slight moisture of a tiny trickle of water
dripping from above.
name: Winding Canyon

desc:
Tall, nearly vertical walls rise up on either side of this valley. The exposed
rock is banded in a thousand layers of tan, orange, brown, and black. Some of
the stone has been dulled by time and erosion, but elsewhere it is quite fresh.
Chunks of rock that have fallen from the walls lie shattered on the ground,
creating a layer of sand and fragile, broken shards.
name: Rocky Valley

desc:
This valley is squeezed between two hills at its northern end. As it progresses
further south, vertical walls of bare stone rise up on either side, until it is
little more than a broad crack in the ground. The slightest sound is magnified
and bounces back and forth between the walls in distorted echoes. The only bit
of vegetation visible is to the north.
name: Narrow Valley

desc:
Unlike the canyon to the south, this fissure was clearly not created through
erosion. The hills have been sundered from one another here, forming a deep
crack between vertical walls. Even at noon, the sun is unable to reach the base
of the fissure, which time and the elements have managed to smooth out with a
layer of sand and broken stones. The crack narrows even further to the west.
name: Narrow, Sandy Fissure

desc:
This portion of the canyon is narrow, and the sky is entirely blocked from sight
by an overhanging shelf of rock, giving the area almost the appearance of a
cave. The ground is rough and uneven, and is littered with chunks and shards of
stone, making any movement treacherous. The walls of the canyon display hundreds
of layers of rock in a remarkable range of widths and colours.
name: Below a Rock Shelf

desc:
This cave runs mainly north and south, but twists as it goes, preventing you
from seeing very far in either direction. The walls of the cave are rounded,
arching until they join seamlessly overhead. The cave's floor is coated in a
thick layer of sand, worn away over time from the stone walls. The cave seems to
open out somewhat to the north.
name: Sandy Cave

desc:
This chamber is quite large, and seems to have been formed by centuries of water
dripping through the ceiling and washing away the stone walls bit by bit. The
floor is coated in thick, wet sand which absorbs sound and muffles noises.
Still, the slow, relentless drip of water can be heard, echoing softly from
every wall. A narrow cave slopes upward to the south.
name: Damp Cavern

desc:
This closet is wide but shallow, being barely large enough for someone to step
inside. It looks like it has not been opened recently, as a thin layer of dust
coats the contents. A set of sturdy pegs has been driven into the wall to allow
a number of items to be hung, but most are empty.
name: Small Closet

desc:
The walls of this cellar are bare earth in most places, while chunks of stone
poke through in others. The floor is solid but slightly uneven. A few crates are
stacked against the walls, kept cool year-round by the ambient temperature of
the ground. A few small holes have been dug into the walls at their base. A
ladder leads upward to a trapdoor in the ceiling.
name: Food Cellar

desc:
This cave runs from west to north, descending quickly into the depths as it
goes. Drops of water fall from dozens of cracks in the ceiling, and trickles run
down every wall, gathering into a thin stream at the centre of the cave. The
floor is smooth and slick, making it treacherous.
name: Sloping Cave

desc:
This entire area is submerged in a lake of dark water that is heavily laden with
minerals leached from the stone overhead. Drops fall in a constant shower from
the ceiling, and a narrow trickle enters the lake from a tunnel to the south.
Echoes bound and rebound from every wall, filling the cavern with noise and
making it difficult to distinguish specific sounds. A broad shelf of stone rises
out of the water to the east.
name: Black Lake

desc:
This shelf of rock stands just above the surface of a black lake to the west. It
is forty or more feet across from north to south, but only half as deep. Walls
of flaking, sandy rock curve upward on every side, merging into a domed ceiling
high over the lake. The only visible way to leave here is across the lake.
name: Broad Shelf

desc:
Slightly narrowing to the north and wider towards the south, the road passes
between houses on either side. White sand with stones of marbled white and black
make the road surface nicely packed over years of use. From the west a constant
smell of baked goods fills the street.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The Old East Road travels north and south here, and is about three yards wide.
This nicely maintained road goes into the trade district in Bree. Several houses
on either side flank the road, their colours contrasting the white gravel and
sand used to make an entirely flat road.
name: Old East Road

desc:
This wide space has become the natural travelling hub of Bree. From the north
music flows, and the pleasant smell of fresh food beckons. The Old East Road
comes from the west and continues south through the village. The Prancing Pony
Inn lies to the north.
name: Sign of the Prancing Pony Inn

desc:
A large front room opens up, where the inn divides further. North lies the
foyer, where rooms can be rented, and west lies the bar, common room, and game
room. The walls and floor are all solid timber frames, clad with wide planks
painted in a pale blue shade on the walls. A few benches for seating stand along
the eastern wall, where the view through a few small windows show the Bree
stables.
name: Prancing Pony Inn

desc:
A risen plateau in the northern part of the Prancing Pony Inn, accessed through
a rounded opening from the south, is where the deals for rented lodging are
made. From here a few rooms can be seen down a corridor, and several stairs lead
into other corridors upstairs.
name: Lodging Foyer

desc:
The Cobble Street narrows going west to east, almost twice as wide towards the
large plaza to the west than continuing east. A large portion of the narrowing
is the entrance to a tall stable north, built onto the large stone slabs the
street is built from.
name: Cobble Street

desc:
As the street made from large stone slabs packed with dirt between them sharply
bends, it intersects with a smaller road heading north. Towards the south the
Cobble Street continues with a small incline towards a tall tower to the south
east.
name: Cobble Street

desc:
The ground is made from packed dirt over years of travelling, with a cover of
sawdust and ground rind from pine trees. The width of the road varies, wider to
the south but narrows slightly as it winds north up a gentle hill. Buildings
accompany the road on both sides, and east lies a large open archery range.
name: Mill Road

desc:
As the road traverses slightly up towards the north it widens, the layer of
sawdust and ground rind on the road is thick and bounces slightly when travelled
upon. It passed between houses on the east side and a man height fence to the
west. Between the posts on the fence one can see a large animal market. Down a
slight descent to the south, the road leads between the stables of Bree and an
archer range.
name: Mill Road

desc:
The road is narrowing both south and west from here, the bend in the Mill Road
is over thirty yards wide. All along the northern part of the road stacks of
untreated logs occupy most of the space, with just enough passing space to pass
by the loggers' cabins and get to the Bree sawmill. A house with several furs
hanging outside is seen to the east.
name: Wide Part of Mill Road

desc:
Passing between stone walls both west and south and wooden houses northeast, the
Cobble Street sharply bends. The stone walls along the southern border stretch
several stories up into the air. Nearby east, a park can be seen. Down a decline
northbound, a stone road, a dirt road and a large stable can be spotted in the
distance.
name: Sharp Bend in the Cobble Street

desc:
Paved with large stones, the Old East Road passes between the walls of the
Prancing Pony inn and tall houses. A pleasant smell of food accompanied by the
noise of people come from the northeast. Straight to the east the road spreads
out to a wide space with a large fountain.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Narrowing slightly, the Old East Road runs both east and west, still a wide
enough road for two wagons to pass each other. North of the road the Bree-hill
rises up, winding through the landscape. Several doors painted in rich colours
are spread out in the hills. South, an open yard spreads out towards a wooden
house.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Winding both east and south, the Old East Road has been paved with large stones,
giving echoes from nearby buildings when horses and wagons pass by. Heading
north, a white gravelled road winds its way upwards into the Bree hills. To the
east several buildings and most prominently the Prancing Pony Inn can be seen.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The wide paved Old East Road continues both west and north from here, and a
narrower Road intersects from the south. To the east a tall fence rises far
overhead, even while mounted. Several buildings can be seen down the south
street, and northwest a stone tower can be seen in the distance.
name: Old East Road

desc:
A narrower stretch of the paved Old East Road, passing between a steeply rising
hill to the north and houses to the south. Any noise here is echoed back from
both directions creating a cacophony of sounds. Westwards the road continues
towards the west gate of Bree.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Stretching both west and east, the wide paved Old East Road passes through the
city of Bree. A small patch of grass ends south of the road in a sloping drop
into a small lake. North a door opens into a small timber house. The west gate
of Bree is nearby, while the city of Bree grows eastwards.
name: Old East Road

desc:
From the solid black building to the north a solid timber wall rises tall up in
the air, featuring a wide wooden gate fixed using metal hinges. The gate has a
latch fixed with a chain, to stop just anyone opening the gate from the inside.
The sound of water can be heard from the south just down a slippery grass slope,
and north a black door leads into the gatekeeper's house. A wooden sign is fixed
to the gate.
name: West Gate of Bree

desc:
The passage between the hedge to the west and the tall gate to the east creates
a fourteen yard wide shaded place. The gates are set shallow in a tall timber
wall, both to the south and north the wall bends out and goes into the hedge,
not allowing any passage. Deep tracks in the ground where the gate has been
opened are clearly visible, showing the gate's frequent use.
name: Outside the West Gate of Bree

desc:
Growing massively thick and tall, the hedge marking the western border of Bree
has been cut to allow travellers through. The opening is over ten yards wide,
just like the road travelling straight west and east. The road widens on either
side of the almost ten yard wide hedge, travelling west towards the Shire and
east to Bree.
name: Under a Large Hedge

desc:
The room is dimly lit, the only light coming from the blazing fireplace, and a
few lamps hanging from the rafters. The bar is just to the right of the
entrance, and a sign has been posted on a pillar beside it. On a good day, this
room is filled with all sorts of travellers and residents of Bree, and many
races can be picked out of the crowds. At times, it can be hard to see in this
room through all the smoke and poor lighting conditions. Behind the bar hangs a
large painting of Bree city.
name: The Prancing Pony Lounge

desc:
This is the main room of the Prancing Pony Inn. A big table dominates the decor,
sitting in the middle of the room surrounded by many smaller tables and chairs.
Often this is the place to witness hobbits, foreigners, and the inhabitants of
Bree talk loudly of the events happening in even the farthest regions of
Middle-earth. Those that rest here long enough may catch snatches of
conversations about faraway places such as Fornost, Rivendell, Tharbad, the Grey
Havens, Moria, and even about the unnamed Shadow that grows in the East.
name: The Common Room

desc:
A small inner room, more silent than the other sitting rooms of the Prancing
Pony Inn, offers some peace and solitude. Four comfortable chairs are set up
beside two tables made to play each other in games. Through a window set high in
the northern wall, Bree-hill can be seen winding north and west. Noise and smell
of food comes in from the east.
name: Game Room

desc:
Equally tall as the neighbouring inn, the stables of Bree are large enough to
house animals of all travellers coming through. Both west and east, eight foot
tall stalls for animals cover the entire side. Above each stall a roof holding
food, equipment and tools for the trade is stored. South through an almost
twenty foot tall opening, a wide stone road can be seen. North lies a separate
market for trading animals.
name: Towering Stable

desc:
A dirt-packed plaza spreads out, housing several stalls and small stables
housing animals for sale. Tenders and owners occupy their own, all deals here
are made through one broker. On the southern end, a tall opening leads into the
Bree stables, and a passing road can be seen north. The fence on the eastern
side shows a large sign and an advisory on pets.
name: Animal Market

desc:
With a slight descent going west from the wide bend in the east, the road winds
its way past an animal market to the south. Packed dirt makes most of the road,
with bits covered in sawdust and rind. Towards the west the road progressively
turns more stone and gravel. In the western horizon the Bree-hill spreads out,
with peaks and valleys en masse.
name: Mill Road

desc:
Turning north and east, the rough road of stone and gravel travels through a dip
in the terrain. A steep hill towards the west bars any climb into the Bree hills
and sliding track attempts to climb can be seen. The road continues northbound
towards a suburb of Bree and east towards the sawmill and the rest of Bree.
name: Stone Road

desc:
As the coarse stone road narrows amply, it passes through an opening in a stone
wall. The massive stone wall coming from the east decreases in height, being
over ten feet on the east end and like a three foot fence when it carries on
west into Bree-hill.
name: Stone Passage

desc:
A wide pathway covered in sawdust and rind passes south and east outside four
equal timber cabins. The cabins hold temporary lodging for all the woodworkers
tending the Bree sawmill when they are not out foresting. The doors are all
locked, but a steady stream of smoke rises from all cabins showing their
occupancy.
name: At the Woodworkers' Cabins

desc:
Stretching fairly straight down south from the Old East Road, this road made
from large stones packed together with dirt passes between several houses on
either side. A coach timetable hangs on a tall eastern wall. A bench provides
rest for tired travellers.
name: Mason Road

desc:
A broad and shallow stream slowly moves westward, watering the nearby hedge
before disappearing under the western walls. The crystalline water flows over
jutting rocks, and cream-coloured sand covers the stream's bottom. The Old East
Road heads further into Bree to the north.
name: Slow Creek

desc:
A clear pond sits among grass and reeds bordering the Old East Road. The water
is deep and calm, and the pond's silty floor is ruffled by darting fish, dashing
to safety from onlookers. To the north, the road heads east and west within
Bree.
name: Placid Pond

desc:
Along the northern and western stone wall, shelves and hooks for storing the
guards' equipment are spread equally. The guards' ready room, where they can
talk freely, play games, and spend a few minutes on break, is solidly built from
stone and wood. A rug of thick braided reeds covers almost the entire room to
dampen noise and soak up moisture.
name: Guards' Station

desc:
About twelve by twelve yards the park stretches, almost flat with round stones
parting walkways and patches for growing plants and herbs. The small fields of
dirt are nicely tended and watered, growing what is fit for each season. Two
small wooden houses stand on the eastern end, smoke is rising from one of them.
Near one of the planted patches a sign is placed.
name: Spice Shop

desc:
Baskets and boxes line up on shelves along the southern wall, some of them have
a lingering pungent smell. On the northern wall, larger items like sacks, water
skins and ropes are on display. The room is narrow with small alcoves on the
eastern side, clearly made for sleeping with pillows and covers ready. A yellow
note is hanging on both sides of the entrance.
name: Bazaar

desc:
Brightly lit and open, divided by long tables made for baking, the room is both
tall and wide. Along the northern wall, several furnaces, warmed by blazing hot
coals and wood, are ready for finalizing dough into the finest bread and
pastries. Placed against the southern wall, underneath a window, a counter shows
baked goods for sale with shelves of fresh bread behind it. A small sign with
text is on the counter.
name: Brightly Lit Bakery

desc:
Tall walls to the north and east and a sloping roof down south and west, create
an uneven house. The wooden floor has one long side to the north and three
shorter equal sides. Large carpets in rich blue and gold colours hang high,
matching the curtains on four wide windows. The inner window edges are painted
artistically showing flowers and growths from the Bree area.
name: Dainty Home

desc:
Bordered by a tall wooden fence on the western side and houses to the east, a
park-like yard spreads out. It is almost entirely flat, rare for the Bree area,
and the grass, flowers and water features seem well tended. Pathways made from
stone slates over white gravel travel outwards from the centre.
name: Open Yard

desc:
An enormous white fur is spread out on the floor, being over nine feet from head
to tail, and makes up the focal point. A whole row of chairs is stacked four in
height along the eastern wall and a large raised patio with a desk is centred at
the western wall, clearly a room used for teaching and meetings.
name: Ranger's Retreat

desc:
Rising from the Old East Road in the south this road traverses northbound in the
hillsides. Even being over ten feet wide it is by no means straight, but winds
its way towards some of the official offices of Bree further north. A small
valley opening westwards leads into an isolated garden further down.
name: Hill Road

desc:
Almost twelve feet wide, this road naturally follows the hills of Bree. It is
made from an almost white gravel, bordered on the sides with larger blocks of
the same stone. Eastwards you can see the roof of the Prancing Pony Inn, and a
door set between two windows in a hillside. To the west the Mayor of Bree and
other official services can be found.
name: Hill Road

desc:
The white gravelled road bordered with white stone blocks flows naturally though
Bree-hill, slightly narrowing where it intersects with an entrance to a large
grassy park to the east. Up on a hillside to the west the wooden wall of a large
house rising up with several ornamented windows is on display.
name: Hill Road

desc:
The white road is only seven feet wide passing through a simple yet sturdy
timber passage, going south it widens to twice that width. The stones framing
the road southbound stop before the passage, allowing the grass to grow close to
the gravel. On either side of the road the Bree-hill rises, creating a shaded
area. North of the passage the road narrows down to a trail made from white
gravel.
name: Hill Road Passage

desc:
Both south and north, steep hillsides create a tranquil and secluded garden,
with a large flat area stretching in all directions. On the southern end of the
flat area a large tent has been erected, and rising up to the west a looming
tower dominates a nearby hilltop. A small opening between the hillsides leads to
a white road in the east.
name: Tranquil Garden

desc:
While grass is growing, this place is mostly covered in green. Most of the hill
is slanting upwards towards the west, before it tips downwards to the Bree
training grounds. When used only muffled sounds make it over the top, making the
grassy hill silent and restful. Sitting down here allows a pleasant rest, while
viewing the busy life of Bree swarm by to the southeast. A few chosen spots have
been used often, where flat areas allow for a campfire and eating a picnic. A
large house rises to the north, through a small opening a desk can be seen.
Heading down east leads to a road in the hills.
name: Bree-hill Park

desc:
The few patches of grass still visible here are struggling with their survival
clearly being stepped on and kicked often. Timber and wooden structures make up
several training apparatus for the guards and others using this room for their
battle readiness. Some are made to climb, others to go under and they seem to be
placed in a circle so one could change the training during an exercise. A few
large timber man-like figures have deep wounds from swords, spears and axes
being slammed into them. A sign reading 'Quartermaster and Warrior Trainer' is
pointing west. North, a stone building housing the guards rises and eastwards
the path leading into Bree can be seen.
name: Training Grounds

desc:
Almost being a part of the training grounds, it still somehow manages to be
barred from most sounds. Two solid and tall timber walls with a tarped roof
being slanted using timber crossbeams allows some cover from both wind and
precipitation. A large number of weapons and armour resembling the ones used by
the Bree guards can be seen on display, covering most of the two walls.
name: Quartermaster's Corner

desc:
Several old campfires can be seen around the hills, where they rise eastwards
towards a park for children. Over some hilltops southwards the roof of the
Prancing Pony Inn can be seen, the smoke from its chimneys crawl though the
bushes growing nearby. In the north hillside a large door with double windows
framed by two windows on each side stick out as a special home.
name: Bree-hill

desc:
With rolling hills rising in the north, east and south a natural flat and shaded
area is used as a city playground. Between two trees, a solid trunk of dark wood
has ropes with large knots hanging, and a long slide has been built into a steep
part of the eastern hillside. A few benches sit alongside a patch of fine sand,
where children's toys are scattered.
name: Hilly Playground

desc:
The manicured stone ground is free from imperfections and bumps, and provides a
safe ground for practicing by the warrior guild members. Tall stone walls
provide cover from wind, and a drain in the yard centre provides a spot for rain
water to collect.
name: Training Yard

desc:
Only the four small windows near the top of the eastern and western stone wall
allow any natural light inside, while several torches hanging on the walls give
it a feeling of evening even during the day. Even the ceiling and the floor are
made from the same stone, the ceiling with a dome-shaped arch to support its
weight.
name: Shaded Stone Building

desc:
This house is built with a timber front, clad in large slabs of wood painted
brightly green on both the inside and outside. Towards the back the house is dug
deep into a hillside, with solid clay covered walls and ceiling. A small kitchen
and a stone clad fireplace cover most of the eastern wall.
name: A Small House

desc:
Built just inside the Bree west gate, this house stands tall and wide. The roof
slopes only northwards, making an impressively tall front towards the Old East
Road. Tall black planks cover the outside, while the insides are painted in a
pale yellow colour.
name: Harry's Manor

desc:
Halfway formed as a hobbit hole, with one and a half wall built from leftover
planks and timber, this burrow is small and narrow. Half of an old barrel make
the foot of a table with an old round door on top. Scattered around the room,
several empty bottles lie discarded, two bottles with candles in them seem to be
the only light source in the room.
name: A Hillside Burrow

desc:
Over fourteen feet in diameter this octagonal tent accommodates all features
needed for comfortable living. In the middle of the tent a large open fireplace
allows for cooking, the smoke rises slowly through an opening in the ceiling
nine feet above the ground. Two timber framed stands hold four hammocks for
sleeping, and a giant bear fur has been carefully put on a deep chair.
name: Heavy Wool Marquee

desc:
Built from a foundation of solid stone five feet high and continuing into a
timber frame this tower stands high. Through a tall arched opening eastwards a
large garden can be seen downhill, and on the eastern wall a solid winding
staircase rises twenty feet up towards a platform.
name: Sorcerer's Stone Tower

desc:
A platform is formed from the inside of the tower, stretching close to the Bree
hedge to the west. The balcony is solidly framed with a railing, walls and a
roof stretching overhead. A few hanging potted plants frame the view west, where
the intersection between the Old East Road and the Greenway can be seen.
name: Shielded Balcony

desc:
This large one-room, once a smial, has been cleverly rebuilt into a nice home
set in the lush Bree-hill. The western wall has two beds built into it, with
heavy woollen covers to draw. The roof is high and shaped like a dome, covered
with a bright red coloured clay. The entire northeastern corner is displaying
tools from a smith, framing a large furnace still able to give off pleasant
heat.
name: Secluded Hobbit Smial

desc:
Two rooms with an open hallway between them form this cosy hobbit home in
Bree-hill. All the walls have been covered with a dimmed pink colour, framed by
green-painted timber. The same green colour is on the round door and the window
frames. A large painting is hanging over a deeply inset fireplace. Smoke from
the fire is flowing out through a wide chimney.
name: Cosy Hobbit Hole

desc:
Only one extra wall is built to house the teaching grounds of Bree. Being set in
a corner with the Bree barracks and jail both east and north, large stone walls
enclose this yard on three sides. The western wall is not nearly as tall as the
other two, only being about six feet. Beyond the western wall the taller green
hedge of Bree can be easily seen, quite a few branches even climb into this
yard.
name: Practice Yard

desc:
Built from stacked stones and isolated with large sacks of packed dirt, this
room dampens the noise from the busy training grounds outside. A large number of
chairs surround two large tables, allowing the Bree city guards a retreat from
the daily rotation. A kitchen with utensils and cups is placed along the eastern
wall, with a large basin of water for cleaning easily available for cleaning.
Through an opening in the stone wall north, an office can be spotted and
southwards through an oblique opening the training grounds can be reached.
name: City Barracks

desc:
The centrepiece of this office is a large desk, made from an elaborate timber
structure. It spans over twelve feet and could easily fit several but only one
man sits at the desk. All visitors are pointed to the bench along the eastern
wall, under a large shelf holding several leather bound books. Several weapons
confiscated from criminals are stacked in a corner, under constant watch from
the guards. The office opens southwards through a stone opening into the guards'
barracks, and a heavy iron gate bars the way into the prison west.
name: Chief of Bree's Office

desc:
This is a long and narrow, yet tall room, built with standing flat planks on the
walls, floor and ceiling. The floor and walls are painted in a bright ochre
colour, with dark green patterns forming trees and a hedge around the room. A
large sign labelled 'Bounty' is hung between the windows on the eastern wall,
alongside a big poster reading 'Citizenship Information'. Outside the office's
south entrance, a large park spreads out, and north another office can be seen.
name: Mayor's Front Office

desc:
Three of the walls here are plain dark ochre-coloured, with the northern wall
featuring a red-painted pattern. A large stack of books in one corner supports a
ladder. The room's centrepiece is a gigantic four-armed cross hanging from the
ceiling, holding torches that light the room.
name: Mayor's Office

desc:
Beautifully erected with white timber covered with dark brown planks on the
outside, this house is completely square in any direction. The white timber
creates a double cross on each wall, and a more intricate pattern of triangles
in the ceiling. A large open fireplace with a chimney is placed on the northern
wall, the chimney holds four large lanterns which light the room.
name: White Timber House

desc:
A well-worn dirt road winds through a remote village nestled deep within the
woods. Small clusters of various wildflowers and overgrown grasses line the road
while flowering dogwoods provide ample shade. To the east, the path begins to
fork much like a river of earth.
name: Dirt Road

desc:
The remote settlement of Archet is made up of a number of stone houses and
smials. The dirt roads that connect the buildings wind through the forest,
eventually meeting at a meticulously crafted cobblestone street that serves as a
makeshift town square near an overgrown barn. One bent and crooked poplar stands
alone in the centre of the street.
name: Poplar on a Cobblestone Street

desc:
A humble fountain constructed of polished river stone sits beneath a massive
weeping willow. Countless bronze and silver coins shimmer at the bottom, a
nameless grave for wishes long since forgotten. Ornate trellises latticed with
wisteria vines form an arcing semi-circle around both the willow and well.
name: Wishing Well

desc:
The dirt road here bisects central Archet. A small tavern can be seen to the
north, while an immaculate hedgerow blocks the way east. Trees, predominantly
ash and elm, dominate the horizon. Deep cart tracks scar the earth, worn deep by
the weight of the annual harvest.
name: Bend in the Dirt Road

desc:
Little more than a dirt path now winds through an abandoned orchard. Dense
cherry trees of unguessable age grow along the deepening forest and give little
choice but to stay on the path. Severe rot and decay blight most of the cherry
branches, starkly contrasting the surrounding luxuriant forest.
name: Abandoned Orchard

desc:
Bordered by a forest clearing to the north and a deteriorating orchard to the
west, several dense rows of hawthorn bushes grow rampant amongst the rolling
hills of Archet. A modest footpath twists through the hawthorns, allowing
passage through the relics of once pristine hedgerows. The shrubbery teems with
countless species of wildlife.
name: Patch of Hawthorns

desc:
Hardly any sky can be seen overhead as thick undergrowth and crowded trees
flourish all around. Chunky mosses and emerald-green ferns carpet the forest
floor, while a humble earthen path provides the only means of escape from the
encroaching woods. A diminutive vernal pond, edged by sedges and rushes, sits
alongside the path and reflects the intertangled canopy above.
name: Forest Clearing

desc:
Bordered on either side by low, wooden buildings, the village trail continues
north and south. To the north, young oak trees appear at the edges of the trail,
thickening into a deep wood on the horizon. To the south, fields of low, bushy,
vibrantly green crops dominate the landscape, except for a large barn that
appears beyond the curve of the trail.
name: End of a Village Trail

desc:
Deep ruts create a ridged pattern on the dirt trail, evidence of many carts
travelling from the barn to the south, through and out of the village. A broken
cart leans against the northern face of the barn and a damp earthy smell
occasionally wafts from that direction.
name: Bend in the Staddle Trail

desc:
Immediately upon entering the barn, a distinct herbal and woody smell overpowers
the senses. High overhead, stalks of pipeweed hang from the rafters of the
ceiling. In the centre of the room are rows of wooden tables with benches at
each end, on top of which dried flowers and leaves lay strewn about after having
been clipped from their stalks.
name: Pipeweed Barn

desc:
Enormous round bales of hay sit perched atop the rolling hills of Archet while
dense thickets of elderberry grow nestled in the valley troughs. A
well-maintained fence built of fresh timber blocks the way north and east,
bounding the fields from the neighbouring woodlands. A twin pair of birch trees,
adorned with gleaming silver-grey bark, grow along the fence.
name: Some Bales of Hay

desc:
A verdant green pasture has been cleared out from the heart of the woods for the
purpose of farming, bordered by a well-maintained fence to the north. A small
pond sits nestled between a group of stately oak trees that nourishes the local
livestock. The surface of the water catches the reflection of the sky above,
afforded by the large break in the forest canopy.
name: Small Pond among the Oaks

desc:
Dozens of mature, thick-trunked cherry trees have been meticulously planted in
neat rows, making for a sizeable orchard. The sweet scent of cherry wood
dominates the space and nary a noise can be heard in any direction. Several reed
baskets in severe disrepair lie littered all around, casualties of long since
forgotten harvests.
name: Cherry Orchard

desc:
Neatly cultivated hills of fertile soil dominate the landscape, nestled tightly
between the Chetwood forest and fields of Bree. The remote village of Archet can
be seen to the northeast, following a snaking dirt road in the distance. Large
amounts of livestock wander about, unfettered by fence or chain, amongst the sea
of tilled fields and emerald grasses.
name: Cultivated Hills

desc:
Lush, well-watered fields have been utilized for the prolific farming of
mushrooms by the locals of Archet. Patches of golden chanterelles and russet
bolete mushrooms grow along decaying elms and countless tufts of rye grass.
Hills and forest begin to make way for an open valley to the south, while a
green barn, overgrown with ivy, crowns a hill to the north.
name: Mushroom Patch

desc:
Scrub and high-growing grass dominate this field. To the west and east, the land
has been eroded by the elements and passage of time, turning it into a series of
downs, with the field occupying the valley between the two. To the northwest,
several large boulders tower above the fields.
name: Fields North of the Grove

desc:
A small grove of birch trees grows here in the fields, possibly leftover from
the Chetwood before it retreated to the north and left this area to the shrubs
and bushes. The trees are spread out enough that they do not form a real forest
or block the sun much. Grass-covered rolling downs stretch out to the west.
name: Birch Grove

desc:
The ground is covered with low shrubs and bushes. The monotony is broken only by
towering boulders which block the way to the west. Far to the north, a thin line
of what might be a road makes its way between a hedge and the Chetwood forest
farther on. A small trail lies on this side of the hedge.
name: Rugged Field

desc:
The wide area is beneath the cliff to the east and lies above several downs
lower to the west. However, the sharp rocks along the edges, which are such a
contrast to this mainly grass-covered spot, prevent further travel to the lands
below. There seems to be no way to move but back up to the hilltop above. There
is also a small opening in the cliff-wall to the east.
name: A Terrace on the Side of the Down

desc:
This small, cramped cave has been naturally cut into the side of the hill
outside over the slow passage of time. The ceiling and walls are damp and slimy.
There are large rocks scattered about, covered in fungus and lichen. The
atmosphere is humid and musty.
name: Deep, Damp Cave in a Hillside

desc:
On the trail leading into Combe, several cow patties have been dried up, as well
as some new ones scattered about the ground. This trail is not used very much,
as evidenced by the lack of wear and tear. There's a smell of fresh crops coming
from somewhere to the east. A rickety door quietly clacks to the west, and the
sound of several cows can be heard faintly, a short distance away.
name: Path to a Logging Trail

desc:
The logging trail extends in an east-west direction while another branch forks
to the south. This crossroad marks the busiest part of the village of Combe.
Each branch of the fork is well maintained at this section of the trail, despite
evidence of heavy use. Small ruts from carriages can be seen in the dirt, as
well as footprints of varying sizes.
name: Crossroads of Combe

desc:
Bordering Combe on the west side, this trail is lightly worn down as it is not
travelled nearly as much as the rest of the village's paths. Bits of tree bark
and fragments litter the ground all about the route from the sporadic logging
carts venturing this way. Further down the path to the east, the trail appears
more worn out. A faint smell of manure fills the air.
name: Lightly-worn Logging Trail

desc:
The stables of Combe can be seen a short distance to the west. A path has been
worn in towards the stables by the endless passing of animals, workers, and
farmhands. Various farming tools have been left scattered about the ground. The
grass is yellowed and patchy from the grazing of animals. The smell of manure
and fertilizer is quite strong in this area.
name: Path before a Stable

desc:
This barn must be at least five yards long by ten yards wide. Bales of hay
dominate the space, with a slew of well-maintained farming tools mounted on the
western wall. It is hard not to sneeze as each crunching step on the hay propels
copious amounts of dust and debris into the air. Several tattered ropes hang
from the rafters and a wooden ladder leads to the barn's loft overhead.
name: Overgrown Green Barn

desc:
This is a simple, yet cosy room. While there is not much in the way of
furniture, a small cot along the northern wall provides a welcome spot to sleep
after a long day's work. Several loaves of bread are cached within a small
cupboard in the corner. A modest study with a fireplace built of river-rock is
to the east. Through a small window, a green barn overgrown with ivy can be seen
to the south.
name: Thatched-roof Cottage

desc:
Here within a modest study sits a fireplace that is built of polished river-rock
and lit with a fire that burns with immense warmth and brightness. Several wolf
furs of obsidian and silver colour lay on the wooden floor, making for a snug
and secure spot to relax. Large bundles of firewood, predominately planks of
cherry and oak, sit piled in the study's corner ready to stoke the fire at a
moment's notice.
name: Modest Study

desc:
A cosy smial has been built into a hill just south of the wishing well and dirt
path. Clearly meant for hobbit residents, this smial is stocked full of
amenities and comforts of every kind. Quaint wooden furniture populates the
room, and small porthole windows peak outwards, providing panoramic views of
Archet. A carved spiral staircase descends downwards in the back corner of the
smial.
name: Cosy Smial

desc:
Dozens of hooped oaken casks full of cherry wine sit here, sweetening with age.
The floor of the cellar is of natural stone, hewn directly into the hill itself.
Two small wooden stools sit here with an ornately carved chessboard placed
between them. A questionably large amount of empty wine glasses lay adjacent to
the board, along with a large scarlet wool blanket.
name: Wine Cellar

desc:
This small tavern provides libations and recreation for the locals, where men
and hobbits both enjoy each other's company. Countless rows of cherry wine
bottles line the barback, along with every sort of liquor imaginable. A single
massive slab of cherrywood acts as the bar, with tall oak stools for seating.
The entire tavern has been decorated from top-to-bottom in a coordinating purple
hue, while a broken chandelier of amethyst and adamant swings precariously
overhead.
name: The Crooked Crocus

desc:
Medium-sized crops grow among the cultivated rows of soil. The field appears to
be actively farmed, as there are no weeds to be seen. The footpaths are worn to
such an extent that they are nearly a foot deeper than the surrounding ground.
name: Fields of Combe

desc:
Many varieties of root crops have been sown here, their long green stems
reaching upwards toward the sky. The farmer has tended the fields well, and
piles of picked weeds can be seen composting along the northern fence. Some
patches of crops have been left bare by birds and rabbits feasting in the area.
The wall running along the eastern side of the fields has been freshly painted
and borders on the path to the farmer's house.
name: Fields of Combe

desc:
The soil in the area has not been ploughed well, it appears mostly desolate and
bare. Rows of crops line the area but have died out and remain stunted. Black
and brown spots can be seen on the leaves, which are also wilting. Mites and
aphids feast upon what little crops remain. Tiny stones, small twigs, and debris
are scattered about the field, and weeds are growing all over. There is a mild
smell of manure coming from the stable to the south.
name: Barren Field

desc:
Several large fields of crops are planted here, each row uniform, growing tall
and towering over one another. Viewing them from a distance, it cannot be
determined where they start nor end. There is a strong smell of upturned soil in
the air, and some dust moves around in the occasional breeze.
name: Fields of Combe

desc:
Inside the stable, there is a powerful smell of hay and manure. The ropes used
to hitch the farm animals to their posts hang on the side of the enclosure. Many
tools are suspended neatly along the eastern wall, under which is a small wagon
whose handle is resting on a bale of hay. Straw covers the ground all about the
stable. Bags of grain and feed can be seen resting on a wooden ledge.
name: Stables of Combe

desc:
Up to a dozen cows can be stored under the shed's roof, each with their own
wooden stall. Broken down bales of hay are placed at ground level for them to
graze on. The stalls are separated by sturdy wooden boards, iron bars, and the
occasional bale of hay stacked up to form a non-permanent wall. Wooden railings
line the length of the shed and serve as tie points for the cows when needed.
Openings in the shed walls allow for aeration and light when it is available.
name: Cow Shed

desc:
Inside the shed, it is surprisingly roomy. Farming gear (spades and sickles,
hammers and hoes, pitchforks and ploughs) is neatly hanging from hooks on the
walls or tucked away in corners. The floor is bare except for a thin layer of
hay scattered unevenly across the ground.
name: Tool Shed

desc:
The pipe-weed plants are much smaller in this field compared to those to the
north and south. They seem more recently planted, and some of the smaller stalks
are attached to short sticks to help them in their upright growth. To the west
lies a large barn with an overhang extending from the roof, under which many
weathered barrels line the exterior wall.
name: Field of Pipe-weed

desc:
The pipe-weed field extends from the west and south, its expansive rows ending
in the east at a track before an enormous barn. Extending from the outer walls
of the barn are low covered tables. Small jars lie strewn across their tops, and
on a few of the tables, tiny seeds, barely larger than grains of sand, litter
the surface.
name: Field of Pipe-weed

desc:
The air is thick with the smell of manure. Piles of dung lay steaming and
stinking at the ends of the rows of pipe-weed, waiting to be distributed to
other areas of the farm. A wooden gate lies directly to the north, and its
adjoining fence extends far to the east.
name: Field of Pipe-weed

desc:
Just to the south, the trail bends and travels alongside a long wooden split
rail fence. A large gate appears on its western edge, with doors sagging
slightly inward. A subtle herbal aroma lingers on the wind. A hedge borders the
trail to the west.
name: Bend in a Village Trail

desc:
The trail follows along a wooden fence, its segments composed of three, sturdy
logs extended horizontally between each post. In some places, the logs have
collapsed due to age and weathering. Small beetles flit between the short
grasses at the edge of the road. A humble cabin stands directly to the north.
name: Village Trail

desc:
Entering the cabin, a large, brick-lined hearth dominates the back wall. Warmth
emanates from the fixture and slowly seeps into the skin. A cast iron pot hangs
from an iron bar near the throat of the chimney and the smell of cooked onion
hangs heavy in the air. A small table abuts the western wall, and a single,
neatly made cot is positioned against the opposite wall. A heap of small animal
furs is piled in the southeast corner.
name: Cosy Cabin

desc:
The pipe-weed bushes grow especially tall in this area, their fuzzy green
flowers extending well over the heads of the hobbits who farm the land. To the
south, a well-groomed hedge borders the field, keeping the precious crop hidden
from prying eyes outside the village.
name: Field of Pipe-weed

desc:
Through the centre of this field of tall pipe-weed plants runs a wide,
tamped-down pathway that leads toward the barn to the north. Small offshoot
paths wind through the fields, allowing farmers to harvest pipe-weed and carry
their yields to the barn for drying and distribution.
name: Field of Pipe-weed

desc:
The rows of densely planted pipe-weed end at a tall hedge in the south. While it
is possible to find a way through the rows, the long leaves fan out into the
makeshift walkways, catching at skin and fabric and leaving behind an
unmistakable herbaceous scent.
name: Field of Pipe-weed

desc:
Perfectly straight rows of tall, shaggy, and vibrantly green pipe-weed bushes
extend far into the southern distance. To the north, a dingy wooden shed leans
ever so slightly on its side; a small glass window placed in the centre of the
shed's wall reveals only darkness beyond.
name: Field of Pipe-weed

desc:
A small but well-worn trail arcs in a wide curve and continues on to the west
and south. Wildflowers grow in thick bunches at the trail's edge. Beyond that,
short grasses extend into the distance, flattened in many places and pocked with
patches of bare dirt in others.
name: Village Trail

desc:
A few torches outside the iron gate leading into this cell makes it dimly lit at
best. The small barred slits inset high in the western wall allow a few beams of
sunlight during daylight hours. Through the slits a wall of green can be
observed and a few branches of the hedge outside almost make it inside the cell.
The walls are all solid stone and the floor is heavily packed dirt, with a few
traces of small animal footprints yet no obvious perpetrator can be seen.
name: Dimly Lit Cell

desc:
The land is nearly flat here, rising only gradually to the west. A tall scarp
rises to the northeast, sheltering this area from some of the worst weather.
Thick grass coats the ground, interspersed with dandelions and daisies in tiny
pools of yellow on a vast green carpet.
name: Sheltered Valley

desc:
The knoll rises gently from the surrounding fields, covered in a thick carpet of
green grass. Small wildflowers in the landscape add pops of colour to the
greenery. The view from the top offers a panoramic view of the surrounding
countryside. The knoll offers great views of the grasslands south and west.
name: Grassy Knoll

desc:
The grassland stretches out as far as the eye can see, the grass rippling gently
in the wind like waves on the sea. Here and there, a small grove of trees
provides a welcome respite from the open expanse. Birds soar overhead, their
cries echoing in the distance. The area is completely enclosed by steep hills,
the only passage is to the north where a verdant knoll is visible.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
These rocky hills offer a stark contrast to the lush hillocks common to the
South Downs. A haphazard tapestry of granite and gneiss, they rise in imposing
grandeur. White spruce and balsam fir saplings take root in the crevices of the
rocky terrain. To the south, the rocky terrain gives way to a solitary ledge, a
protruding platform of stone. To the west, the landscape transforms into
windswept hillocks.
name: Rocky Line of Hills

desc:
This moist den is covered in wet leaves and mud. A few depressions have been
made in one corner, perhaps caused by some large beast rolling over in its sleep
there. A flap of packed mud on the western wall marks the only exit to this
tight chamber.
name: Muddy Bear Den

desc:
The entrance to the cave in the west is partially concealed by a tangle of
brambles and gnarled roots and extends into a narrow passageway that winds its
way towards the South Down hills. A faint, eerie glow emanates from a cluster of
bioluminescent fungi that clings to the walls. To the east, a stone wall riddled
with crevices looms large.
name: Small Cave

desc:
The cave's naturally formed walls, displaying a smooth and straight appearance,
evidently bear the marks of human craftsmanship. Dirty items of contraband
litter the floor, while boxes and barrels stand empty, ready to be filled with
ill-gotten gains. To the west, a narrow passage beckons the curious, a sinuous
crack in the rock just wide enough for a slender frame to slip through. To the
north, the wall is adorned with strange markings.
name: Smuggler's Hiding Place

desc:
The chamber resembling a crack in the cave's rock serves a significant function
as the smuggler's treasure storage, despite its diminutive size. This unassuming
nook seems to be home to the smuggler's most valued treasures, yet the rough
walls and uneven floor suggest that little work was put into its construction.
name: Smuggler's Treasure Room

desc:
The land here is a series of steep hills, their slopes dressed in a motley garb
of bracken and heather. The slopes are bedecked in a rich tapestry of northern
bedstraw and Labrador tea, their delicate blossoms a stark contrast against the
sweeping emerald. Trees, proud and mighty, emerge from the undulating sea of
grass, their roots delving deep into the rich, loamy soil of the slopes.
name: Verdant Hillocks

desc:
Thin slices of sharp shale break the ground's monotony, their jagged edges
protruding like the dorsal fins of ancient leviathans. The few oaks here stand
tall and proud, their roots plunged deep into the fertile soil. Curled around
the boulders, vibrant ferns unfurl their fronds. To the south, a verdant hillock
rises gently. East and west, steep hill slopes stretch upward and make travel
impossible. Northward, a meadow unfurls like a green carpet, dotted with the
vibrant hues of wildflowers.
name: Mouth of a Narrow Valley

desc:
In the heart of the forest lies a peaceful meadow, surrounded by hills. The
grass is soft and inviting, and wildflowers grow in abundance. The meadow serves
as a basin that is fed by numerous streams, each flowing down from the peaks,
merging into larger and deeper watercourses. The meadow rises into rocky hills
to the north. To the south, a mouth of a narrow valley is visible.
name: Meadow below Some Steep Hills

desc:
The thick smell of large, wet, mammals permeates this dank cavern. Large
scratches cover the walls and floor, perhaps from the same creatures sharpening
their claws. A small pile of the remains of crushed nuts and berries cover one
corner, long since devoured.
name: Musky Cavern

desc:
The ground of this rocky ledge is a mosaic of bare stone and moss-covered rock.
From this vantage point, a verdant, hill-ringed valley stretches out in the
distance. A small hill rises from the valley floor, its form covered in a carpet
of grass. Mosses and lichens find purchase in the crevices of the rock, their
forms tracing the contours of the stone.
name: Rocky Ledge

desc:
A skilled craftsman has meticulously transformed the grotto's walls, which were
once jagged and rough, into smooth surfaces. Within the grotto, various crates
and barrels are carefully arranged, indicating that this space has been used as
a warehouse or a hideout. To the east, the grotto continues into a stone-lined
passage. To the west, a large stone slab, perfectly fitted to the grotto's wall,
conceals an exit leading to the South Downs.
name: Carved Grotto

desc:
Nestled along the passage, wooden cupboards, crates, and barrels are neatly
arranged, suggesting the space's previous use as a warehouse or hideout. The
scent of damp earth and moss fills the nostrils. The sound of water dripping
from stalactites and echoing off the walls create a rhythmic, almost melodic
symphony. The passage continues toward the west and the east.
name: Stone-lined Passage

desc:
The walls of the hollow are a mixture of raw, jagged stone and smooth, carved
surfaces, evidence of both nature's hand and the skilled touch. Crates and
barrels line the walls and tables and chairs are scattered throughout the space,
worn by time and use. In the centre of the hollow, a stone table stands, its
surface engraved with markings that look like some sort of a map. The only exit
from the hollow is to the west, a subtle crack in the rocky facade, adorned with
intricately woven vines that blend seamlessly with the surrounding environment.
name: Rocky Hollow

desc:
The chamber is littered with tattered bedrolls, broken cookware, and crude
wooden tables. Narrow bunks of spartan design line two walls, their mattresses
filled with straw and covered in tattered blankets. A long table runs between
them, with sturdy wooden benches to either side, worn smooth from countless
occupants. Simple cabinets stand against the far wall. To the south, a door with
rusty hinges leads to a storage room. The wooden wall to the east bears the
marks of age, with cracks and fissures running through its rough surface.
name: Ascetic Quarters

desc:
On the rough walls of this warehouse, an array of cupboards and wooden shelves
stand. The air is thick with the scent of damp soil and lingering traces of pipe
smoke. Cobwebs drape the ceiling, revealing the passage of time and the lack of
housekeeping. The floor is uneven and strewn with a curious mix of hay and
sawdust. To the north, a hinged door leads to living quarters.
name: Cramped Warehouse

desc:
The vast chamber is filled with the remnants of plunders. Cobwebs drape over
countless crates. Elaborate sconces, now devoid of flame, line the walls. In the
centre of the cavern, an enormous stalactite hangs like a frozen teardrop, its
surface glistening with moisture. Water drips incessantly from the ceiling,
pooling in the crevices and nourishing a carpet of luminescent fungi. To the
west, a small exit leads to the living quarters.
name: Abandoned Cavern
